title: The Optimality of the Interleaving Distance on Multidimensional Persistence Modules
speaker: Michael Lesnick
affiliation: Stanford University; Member, School of Mathematics, IAS
abstract: Persistent homology is a central object of study in applied topology. It offers a flexible framework for defining invariants, called barcodes, of point cloud data and of real valued functions. Many of the key results of the last several years in the theory of persistent homology have been formulated in terms of a metric on barcodes called the bottleneck distance. There is a multi-parameter generalization of persistent homology, called multi-dimensional persistent homology, which is naturally suited to the study of noisy point cloud data.

proof_stages.label: Barcode-valued persistent homology intuition
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces ordinary persistent homology as the passage from data or a real-valued function to a one-parameter filtration and then to a barcode recording the births and deaths of persistent holes.

proof_stages.label: Distance-based stability motivation
proof_stages.summary: Distances are framed as extended pseudometrics, and the bottleneck distance on barcodes is presented as the metric underlying stability, approximation, and inference results in ordinary persistence.

proof_stages.label: Multidimensional distance problem
proof_stages.summary: Multidimensional persistent homology is motivated as a richer invariant whose output is a persistence module rather than a barcode, leading to the central question of choosing a principled distance and the informal claim that the interleaving distance is optimal.

proof_stages.label: Formal n-parameter persistence modules
proof_stages.summary: The speaker sets up the coordinatewise partial order on Z^n, defines n-dimensional filtrations and sublevel-set filtrations, and applies homology functorially to obtain n-dimensional persistence modules.

proof_stages.label: One-dimensional barcode theorem and higher-dimensional obstruction
proof_stages.summary: Using morphisms, interval modules, direct sums, and pointwise finiteness, the one-dimensional structure theorem is recalled, after which the complexity of higher-dimensional indecomposables explains why a satisfactory multidimensional barcode is unavailable.

proof_stages.label: Bottleneck distance and stable-distance axiom
proof_stages.summary: The bottleneck distance is explained via matching and deforming intervals, then stability is formalized as the requirement that module distance be bounded by the sup-norm distance between input functions, recovering the standard bottleneck stability theorem in one dimension.

proof_stages.label: Formal optimality theorem
proof_stages.summary: A distance on n-dimensional persistence modules is defined to be optimal when it is stable and dominates every other stable distance, and the theorem states that the interleaving distance has this property over Q or finite prime fields.

proof_stages.label: Interleaving distance, isometry, and consequences
proof_stages.summary: Epsilon-interleavings are defined as compatible shifted maps between persistence modules, the interleaving distance is their infimal shift, its equality with the bottleneck distance in one dimension is recalled, and the proof strategy and inference-theoretic upshot of optimality are summarized.

examples.name: Two-ring point cloud and its barcode
examples.note: A point cloud consisting of a large sparse ring and a small dense ring is used to make concrete how union-of-balls filtrations produce long barcode intervals for persistent cycles and short intervals for noise.
examples.engagement_reason: he runs the union-of-balls filtration on the two-ring cloud and traces when the two holes are born and die in the barcode

examples.name: Naive staircase building blocks
examples.note: Hypothetical staircase-shaped regions of copies of the field are shown as the tempting but inadequate higher-dimensional analogue of interval summands, clarifying why barcode-like decompositions fail.

examples.name: Noisy nearby real-valued functions
examples.note: Two close functions on the real line, one with small bumps, illustrate the stability theorem by showing that small sup-norm perturbations add only short barcode intervals that can be collapsed.
